coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodot modified N, O-doped porous carbon for solid-state supercapacitor: A comparative study with carbon nanotube and graphene oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yi
Quan, Hongying
Cui, Jingmiao
Luo, Wansheng
Zeng, Weiliang
Chen, Dezhi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a series of nanocarbons, including carbon nanodots (CD), carbon nanotubes (CNT) and graphene oxide (GO), modified N, O doped hierarchical porous carbons (NOHPC) were prepared by carbonization using the silica nanospheres and ZnCl2 as templates. The N, O doping content, N, O species and the ratio of micro/mesopore pore structures of NOHPC can be effectively improved by the modification of nanocarbons in organic precursor of polyacrylonitrile. The as-prepared CD modified NOHPC (CD/NOHPC) showed superior capacitance performance than that of CNT (CNT/NOHPC) and GO (GO/NOHPC), and the CD/NOHPC exhibited a high specific capacitance of 343.6 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and an excellent rate capability (304.7 F g−1 at 50 A g−1 and 88.7% capacitance retention). The symmetric supercapacitor of CD/NOHPC delivered an energy density of 10.3 Wh kg−1 (0.31 mWh cm−3) at a power density of 489 W kg−1 (14.7 mW cm−3), a capacitance retention of 109% after 20000 galvanostatic charge/discharge cycles in H2SO4/PVA solid-state electrolyte. The systematic study clarified that CD can be utilized for the regulation and improvement of the internal structure of traditional porous carbon materials, resulting in better electrochemical energy storage than that of CNT and GO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supercapacitor
N-doping
Hierarchical porous carbon
Composite

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 51968049 , 52063023 ) of China, the Youth Science Foundation (Grant No. 20192ACB21031 , 20202ACBL204012 ) of Jiangxi Province, China, the Young Talents Training Plan (Grant No. 20192BCB23012 ) for Scientific and Technological Innovation of Jiangxi Province, China.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites.
Typical SEM images of a) NOHPC, b) CD/NOHPC, c) CNT/NOHPC and d) GO/NOHPC.
Typical TEM and HRTEM images of a1 and a2) NOHPC, b1 and b2) CD/NOHPC, c1 and c2) CNT/NOHPC and d1 and d2) GO/NOHPC.

a) Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms, b) pore-size distribution curves, c) Smicro@Smeso, Vmicro@Vmeso histograms and d) ratio of Smicro/Smeso and Vmicro/Vmeso curves of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites.
XPS spectra of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites. a) survey scan, b) N1s, c) O1s and d) atomic% of N and O species.

Electrochemical properties of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites. a) CV curves at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1, b) CV curves of CD/NOHPC at scan rates from 10 to 200 mV s−1, c) the linear relationship between the charge/discharge current densities and scan rates, d) GCD curves at a current density of 1 A g−1, e) GCD curves of CD/NOHPC at current densities from 1 to 50 A g−1, f) specific capacitance of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites at different current densities (calculated from the discharge curves).
a) Nyquist plots and b) Bode phase plots of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites.

Electrochemical performance of NOHPC and NOHPC composites SSCs. a) CV curves at 5 mV s−1, b) CV curves of CD/NOHPC SSC at different scan rates, c) GCD curves at 1 A g−1, d) GCD curves of CD/NOHPC SSC at different current densities, e) specific capacitance as a function of current density, f) Ragone plots of assembled SSCs, and g) cycling performance of CD/NOHPC- SSC at a current density of 5 mA cm−1.
a) CV curves of CD/NOHPC SSC with different bending angle at 200 mV s−1; b) Lighting devices connected by three CD/NOHPC SSCs.
Schematic diagram of synthesis process of NOHPC composites.
Pore characteristics of NOHPC, CD/NOHPCs, CNT/NOHPCs and GO/NOHPCs.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Total specific surface area (SBET), micropore surface area (Smicro) were calculated using the Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET) method and the t-plot method, respectively.
Total pore volume (Vtotal) and micropore volume (Vmicro) were determined using the density functional theory (DFT).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The element composition (atom %) of C, N, O in NOHPC and NOHPC composites from analysisi of the XPS spectra.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodot modified N, O-doped porous carbon for solid-state supercapacitor: A comparative study with carbon nanotube and graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Jiangxi Province for Persistent Pollutants Control and Resources Recycle, School of Environmental and Chemical Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang Hangkong University, Nanchang 330063, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a series of nanocarbons, including carbon nanodots (CD), carbon nanotubes (CNT) and graphene oxide (GO), modified N, O doped hierarchical porous carbons (NOHPC) were prepared by carbonization using the silica nanospheres and ZnCl2 as templates. The N, O doping content, N, O species and the ratio of micro/mesopore pore structures of NOHPC can be effectively improved by the modification of nanocarbons in organic precursor of polyacrylonitrile. The as-prepared CD modified NOHPC (CD/NOHPC) showed superior capacitance performance than that of CNT (CNT/NOHPC) and GO (GO/NOHPC), and the CD/NOHPC exhibited a high specific capacitance of 343.6 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 and an excellent rate capability (304.7 F g−1 at 50 A g−1 and 88.7% capacitance retention). The symmetric supercapacitor of CD/NOHPC delivered an energy density of 10.3 Wh kg−1 (0.31 mWh cm−3) at a power density of 489 W kg−1 (14.7 mW cm−3), a capacitance retention of 109% after 20000 galvanostatic charge/discharge cycles in H2SO4/PVA solid-state electrolyte. The systematic study clarified that CD can be utilized for the regulation and improvement of the internal structure of traditional porous carbon materials, resulting in better electrochemical energy storage than that of CNT and GO.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors have become one of the most promising energy storage devices because of their high power density, long cycle life and low maintenance cost [1,2]. Electrochemical double layer capacitors (EDLCs), mostly using carbon based materials as electrode materials, are the most common supercapacitors at present due to the advantages of high stability and low cost [3,4]. Among available carbon materials, activated carbon is the most frequently used electrode material for EDLCs due to its high specific surface area (SSA), environmental compatibility and low-cost [5,6]. Typically, activated carbon, when prepared using suitable precursors and activators, possesses an ultrahigh SSA up to 2000–4000 m2 g−1 with abundant micropores[7]. However, because of the limited ion accessible area and sluggish ion diffusion, it is difficult to achieve a satisfactory specific capacitance and rate performance in the micropore dominated structure. Therefore, carbon materials, which have a rational porous structure with full use of high SSA, are urgently required as an ideal electrode material for high-performance EDLCs [8–13].

Hierarchical porous carbons (HPCs), containing interconnected micro-, meso- and macropores, have been developed to overcome the shortage of micropore dominated structures since their hierarchical porous architectures can effectively improve the ion-accessibility of the pores and accelerate ion diffusion [14–17]. In addition, heteroatom doping has been developed to further promote the capacitance performance of HPCs because of their beneficial surface modification and pseudocapacitance contribution [9,10,16,18–22]. Among various heterogeneous doped HPCs, N, O doped hierarchical porous carbon (NOHPC) has attracted wide attention in supercapacitors due to its high specific surface area, good wettability, abundant raw materials, and interconnected porous structure [10,11,18,23,24].

Recently, composites, that comprised of different kinds of carbons, such as carbon nanodots (CD), carbon nanotubes (CNT) and graphene oxide (GO) with different structural dimensions and/or porous structures, have been fabricated to improve the performance of EDLCs through the structure promoted synergistic effects [25–28]. For example, CD modified activated carbon electrodes demonstrated a specific capacitance of 0.185 F cm−2, which is three time higher than the capacitance of unmodified activated carbon [29]. An interconnected network of macroporous graphene/ordered mesoporous carbon exhibited excellent electrochemical properties as electrode materials in supercapacitors [30]. Zhang et al. [31] developed graphene quantum dots as the structural units to synthesize ultramicroporous carbons through chemical welding and in-situ activation, and the obtained material delivered excellent capacitance properties. Wang et al. [32] fabricated hierarchical porous carbon nanotubes (HPCNTs) by a simple carbonization treatment without any assistance of soft/hard templates and activation procedures, the HPCNTs realized high efficiency energy storage due to their unique internal space gap. Lyophilized graphene oxide-chitosan (GO-CS) composite hydrogels-derived three-dimensional rGO/hierarchical porous carbon composites (3DHCG) exhibited a high specific capacitance of 320 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 in 6 M KOH solution [33]. Activated carbon-coated carbon nanotubes showed a high areal capacitance up to 3.6 F cm−2 at a scan rate of 2 mV s−1 in aqueous 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte [28]. Although the significant research results had been achieved in earlier studies, the systematic investigation of the composite or co-doping effect of CD, CNT and GO and hierarchical porous carbon materials in supercapacitors is still lacking.

Herein, CD modified NOHPC (CD/NOHPC) was prepared by carbonization of carbon nanodots and polyacrylonitrile using the silica nanospheres and ZnCl2 as templates. For comparison, the carbon nanotubes (CNT) modified NOHPC (CNT/NOHPC) and graphene oxide (GO) modified NOHPC (GO/NOHPC) were also fabricated. The pore structures, the ratio of micro/mesopore, the content of N atom and N doping types of three nanocarbons modified NOHPC composites were investigated in detail. As electrode materials for supercapacitors, the specific capacitances of the as-prepared CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC were 343.6, 273.9 and 308.1 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 in 1 M H2SO4 solution, respectively, which is superior than that of bare NOHPC (232.0 F g−1). The uniformly modified various nanocarbons integrated with N, O doping played a crucial role in the enhancement of capacitance performance of NOHPC. Importantly, the solid-state supercapacitor (SSC) of CD/NOHPC delivered an energy density of 10.3 Wh kg−1 (0.31 mWh cm−3) at a power density of 489 W kg−1 (14.7 mW cm−3) and an excellent cycling stability (a high capacitance retention of 109% over 20,000 cycles) in H2SO4/PVA solid-state electrolyte. Our work contributes to a better understanding of CDs modification as an effective strategy to boost the capacitance performance of porous carbon materials.

CD aqueous solution was synthetized using the reported method (Section S1) [34]. Multi-walled CNT (TNM8) aqueous solution was purchased from Chengdu Organic Chemicals Co. Ltd., Chinese Academy of Sciences. GO aqueous solution was prepared by our previous approach (Section S2) [35]. The typical TEM images of CD, CNT and GO are shown in Fig. S1.

The composites (CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC) were prepared by the following process. First, 6 g of ZnCl2 was completely dissolved in 10 mL of GO, CD and CNT solution (1.0 mg mL−1) under constant stirring. Then, 0.5 g of polyacrylonitrile (PAN, precursor) and 0.25 g of nano-silica (15
± 5 nm )
was added to the above solution slowly under continuously stirring for 2 h. The obtained suspension was freeze-dried for 24 h. Subsequently, the dry product was heated to 280 ℃ with a heating rate of 1 ℃ min−1 under air for 1 h and then heated to 900 ℃ with a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1 under N2 for 0.5 h. After cooled to room temperature, the obtained black powder was impregnated in HF aqueous solution (30 wt%) for 12 h to remove the silica template and residual Zn, and then washed using deionized water until the pH to 7. Finally, the as-prepared composites were dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h. For comparison, the bare NOHPC was prepared using the same procedure without nanocarbons.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) (FEI Quanta 250) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Talos F200X) was applied to observe the morphology and structure of the as-prepared samples. X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD) (Bruker, D8 Advance) was used to analyze the phase of the samples. The graphitization degree and defect in the samples were studied by Raman spectrometer (LabRAM HR800). An Axis Ultra X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to characterize the element and chemical state on the surface of the samples. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm, recorded on a TriStar II 3020 instrument (Micromeritics) at 77 K, was utilized to investigate the specific surface area and pores size distributions of the samples.

The electrochemical properties of the samples were characterized by cyclic voltammogram (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD) and the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in a three-electrode system using 1 M H2SO4 solution as electrolyte, Pt foil and Ag/AgCl electrode as the counter and the reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode was fabricated using the following process: as-prepared samples, polyvinylidene fluoride and conductive carbon black with a mass ratio of 8:1:1 were mixed in poly-2-methyl-pyrrolidone under continuous stiring to obtain a homogeneous slurry, then the slurry was coated on the surface of carbon cloth (CC) and dried at 80
℃ for 24 h
. The mass loading of as-prepared samples on CC was about 3 mg cm−2. For solid-state supercapacitors (SSCs), two symmetric working electrodes were assembled using a filter paper as separator and 1 M H2SO4/PVA as solid electrolyte.

The gravimetric capacitance of the as-prepared samples in both three-electrode system and SSCs was calculated from the Formula (1). The volumetric capacitance, the energy density and power density of SSCs was calculated from the Formula (2), Formula (3) and Formula (4) respectively.
(1)
C
s
=
I × ∆ t
m × ∆ V
(2)
C
v
=
I × ∆ t
v × ∆ V
(3)
E =
1
2 × 3.6
C
∆ V
2
(4)
P =
E × 3600
∆ t
Where the C
s (F g−1) and C
v (F cm−3) are the gravimetric and volumetric specific capacitance, respectively. I (A) is the applied current, Δt (s) is the discharge time, m (g) is the mass loading of the as-prepared samples on electrode, v (cm−3) is the volume of assembled SSC, ΔV (V) is the working potential range, E (Wh kg−1 or mWh cm−3) and P (W kg−1 or mW cm−3) are the energy density and the power density of assembled SSC, respectively.

Scheme 1 illustrates the preparation of NOHPC composites. ZnCl2 and nano-silica were used to form micro- and mesopores, respectively. Because of the sublimation of water and the release of gas, macropores appeared during the freeze-drying process and carbonization. Furthermore, accounting the value of the melting point (317 ℃) of PAN precursor, the pre-carbonization temperature was selected at 280 ℃. During pre-carbonization in the atmosphere, the precursor slowly expanded resulting in the increase of the specific surface area and porosity of the as-prepared carbon materials to improve their electrochemical performance. Meanwhile, the pre-carbonization could effectively preserve N species in precursor, which can realize the high N doping in the as-prepared carbon materials and promote electrochemical performance. Fig. 1a shows the XRD patterns of NOHPC and NOHPC composites. The diffraction patterns demonstrate two broad peaks around 2θ = 26 and 44°, indicating that these as-prepared carbon materials are amorphous. The high intensity at the low diffraction angle suggested the existence of abundant pores in NOHPC and NOHPC composites [36]. Fig. 1b displays the Raman spectra of the NOHPC and NOHPC composites, which present a D band located around 1342 cm−1 and a G band around 1595 cm−1. The D band is attributed to the defects or disordered carbon structure, while the G band corresponds to the ordered graphitized sp2 carbon. The intensity ratio of D band and G band (ID/IG) is usually employed to evaluate the degree of disorder and the defect density of carbon materials. The values of ID/IG of NOHPC, CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC are 1.08, 1.20, 1.18 and 1.18, respectively, indicated that the introduction of theses nanocarbons into a precursor could increase the disorder degree and the content of defects in NOHPC materials.

Fig. 2 presents the typical SEM images of pristine NOHPC and NOHPCs composites. From Fig. 2a, the NOHPC, which was comprised of loose laminated carbon skeletons and abundant macropores, showed sponge-like structures. Compared with pristine NOHPC, the sponge-like structures of CD/NOHPC (Fig. 2b) became more dense and uniform since the induction of highly dispersed zero-dimensional CD. In addition, some chain-shaped structures were observed in CNT/NOHPC sponge because of the one-dimensional characteristics of CNT (Fig. 2c). In GO/NOHPC (Fig. 2d), some sheet-like structures appeared. Fig. 3 exhibits the typical TEM and HRTEM images of NOHPC and NOHPC composites. From the TEM images (Figure 3a1-d1), a sponge-like structure with abundant interconnected meso- and micro-pores can be clearly observed. The disorder lattice stripes in Figure 3a2 indicates that the NOHPC is amorphous. Although the original ordered crystal structures of CD and CNT were destroyed because of the activation process of ZnCl2, the lattice stripes with spacing of 0.34 nm, indexed to the (002) crystal plane of crystalline carbon, can still be observed in the HRTEM images of CD/NOHPC (Figure 3b2) and CNT/NOHPC (Figure 3c2). In addition, the morphology of GO/NOHPC (Figure 3d2) at the edges site is more clearly affected by the graphene-based nanosheets and edge wrinkles.

Fig. 4a displays the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of pure NOHPC and NOHPC composites, which are assigned to a type IV isotherm with H4 hysteresis ring between P/P0 = 0.45 and 1. The steep increase of the adsorbed volume at low P/P0 value of 0–0.03 indicated the presence of micropores. In addition, the subsequent slope observed at medium relative pressure and the desorption hysteresis showed the occurrence of abundant mesopores. The pore-size distribution curves (Fig. 4b) further confirmed the presence of micropores and mesopores in bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites. The hierarchical micro-mesopore structure is beneficial to the migration and penetration of ions in the electrolyte to the electrode, thereby enhancing the kinetics of the electrochemical reaction [37]. Table 1 lists the specific surface area and pore volume of the samples. The SBET of CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC was 1384, 1343 and 1434 m2 g−1, respectively, which larger than that of NOHPC (1207 m2 g−1). The Smicro of NOHPC, CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC was 68, 172, 140 and 140 m2 g−1, respectively. It indicated that the addition of CD, CNT and GO in precursor did not occupy the micropores of NOHPC, but increased the pore for the composites, which is consistent with the reference work that the composites of CD and microporous ZIF-8 exhibited a larger BET surface area than bare ZIF-8 [38]. In our work, the increasing micropore may be attributed to that the modification of CD, CNT and GO could effectively prevent collapse of the pore structure in NOHPC, and the sublimated Zn also can make micropore in CD, CNT and GO. Among all samples, the CD/NOHPC possessed the highest ratio of Smicro/Smeso and Vmicro/Vmeso (Fig. 4d). The increased micropores in NOHPC composites could effectively increase the active sites for EDLC. Compared with bare NOHPC, NOHPC composites showed higher surface area, which manifesting that modification with nanocarbon of the precursor is a effective approach to regulate the pore structure without sacrificing the original pore structure of the NOHPC.

Since the EDLC mainly depends on the interface reaction between the electrode materials and the ions in the electrolyte, the surface groups of the electrode materials are of great significance to their capacitance performance [34]. Fig. 5 demonstrates the XPS spectra of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites. The XPS survey spectra show three distinct characteristic peaks at 285, 399 and 532 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. The N1s spectra in Fig. 5b can be deconvoluted into four components at around 398.3, 399.8, 401.0 and 403.8 eV, which are attributed to pyridine N (N-6), pyrrole N (N-5), graphitized N (N-G) and oxidized N (N-O), respectively. The O1s spectra (Fig. 5c) can be divided into three peaks around 530.8, 532.4 and 533.8 eV, which correspond to C=O, C–O and COO, respectively. Furthermore, a peak centered at about 535.8 eV in NOHPC and CD/NOHPC is assigned to the physically adsorbed H2O and O2.

The atomic percentages of C, O and N in the surface of as-prepared NOHPC and NOHPC composites were calculated from the XPS spectra and shown in Fig. 5d and Table 2. The N contents of CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC were 6.88, 5.00 and 6.12 at%, higher than that of NOHPC (3.98 at%), indicated that the adding of nanocarbons in precursor could be used as host to reserve the N species [39,40]. The different content of N doping in these composites may be attributed to the affinity between N source and the surface of nanocarbons. The high N doping content have been considered to be advantageous to improve the supercapacitor performance of electrode materials [30,41]. Generally, N-6 is an electron-donor and can enhance the redox activity in electron transfer reactions, resulting in the improvement of capacitance of electrode material [37]. Furthermore, N-5 can provide certain pseudocapacitance, N-G promotes electronic transfer kinetics and enhances the conductivity of the electrode material, N-O can improve the hydrophilicity of carbon materials [42]. Fig. S2 displays the dynamic water contact angle measurement of the as-prepared samples. The contact angles between water droplets and the surface of samples indicated that the hydrophilicity order was CD/NOPHC> GO/NOHPC> CNT/NOHPC> NOHPC. In particular, the atomic percentages of N-5 and N-6 in CD/NOHPC (Fig. 5d) were obviously higher than those of other samples. The higher N-6 and N-5 can provide more active sites for energy storage. On the other hand, the O doping in the surface of carbon materials also can improve their capacitance performance. Generally, the quinone-like C–O and the pyrone-like C=O groups are benefited to boost the specific capacitance, while the effect of COO is in the opposite way [43]. From Fig. 5d, the total contents of C=O and C–O in bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites were almost the same, but the COO of CD/NOHPC was far lower than other samples, suggesting that the positive effect of oxygen-containing groups in CD/NOHPC was higher than that of NOHPC and other NOHPC composites. Moreover, the total content of N–6, N–5, C–O and C=O also is shown in Fig. 5d, and the CD/NOHPC possessed higher value than other samples, implying its higher capacitance performance than other samples.

The electrochemical properties of as-prepared bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites were investigated in a three-electrode system using 1 M H2SO4 as electrolyte. All the CV curves ( Fig. 6a) of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites electrodes exhibited a quasi-rectangular with near mirror-image symmetry, suggesting an idea EDLC capacitive behavior. In addition, a weak reversible hump near the voltage of 0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl indicated that the presence of pseudocapacitance originated from the heteroatom doping such as oxygen and nitrogen. The integrated area of the CV curve for CD/NOHPC was larger than those of pure NOHPC and other NOHPC composites, suggesting the higher capacitance of CD/NOHPC. Fig. 6b exhibits the CV curves of CD/NOHPC at different scan rates, remaining a rectangular shape without any distortion even at a high scan rate of 200 mV s−1. It suggests that the CD/NOHPC electrode had high rate performance and charge-propagation capability. A good linear relationship between the charge/discharge current densities and scan rates shown in Fig. 6c indicated the main EDLC contribution for the CD/NOHPC electrode. To further estimate the electrochemical properties of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites, GCD curves were obtained at a current density of 1 A g−1 and shown in Fig. 6d. All these GCD curves were nearly linear and the charge curves were symmetrical to the corresponding discharge curves, further confirming the idea capacitance behavior of the NOHPC and NOHPC composites electrodes. Notably, a slight offset around the voltage of 0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl is due to the pseudocapacitance contribution of N and O in the material [44], which are consistent with the CV curves. The GCD offset of CD/NOHPC is more obvious, suggesting that the pseudocapacitance contribution of CD/NOHPC was higher than that of pure NOHPC and other composites. Fig. 6e and Fig. S3 demonstrate the GCD curves of CD/NOHPC and other samples at different current densities, respectively. According to the galvanostatic discharge curves, the calculated specific capacitance plots are shown in Fig. 6f. The specific capacitances of NOHPC, CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC were 232.0, 343.6, 273.9, and 308.1 F g−1 at current density of 1 A g−1, respectively. The as-prepared NOHPC composites delivered enhanced specific capacitances after modified with nanocarbons, especially CD. Furthermore, the capacitance retention of CD/NOHPC was 88.7% even at a high current density of 50 A g−1 (304.7 F g−1 at 50 A g−1), which was superior to CNT/NOHPC (88.1%), GO/NOHPC (87.2%) and NOHPC (83.9%). It clearly indicates that zero-dimensional CD was more favorable to adjust the internal structure to form uniformly distribution in the porous carbon skeleton of NOHPC, which could provide better structure-promoted effect on porous carbon edge [45] than one-dimensional CNT and two-dimensional GO, thus resulting in the excellent electrochemical performance of the CD/NOHPC. Moreover, compared with other NOHPC samples, CD/NOHPC exhibited the higher N, O doping, especially the higher content of N-6, N-5, C-O and C˭O, which is helpful for the improvement of electrochemical activity of carbon materials [46,47].

The EIS measurement was carried out to study electronic conductivity of bare NOHPC and NOHPC composites. The obtained Nyquist plots in 1 M H2SO4 are depicted in Fig. 7a. Generally, Nyquist plot is composed of three characteristic regions. At high frequency region, the intercept to real axis corresponds to the equivalent series resistance (Rs) of the electrode electrochemical system. At middle frequency region, the presented semicircle represents charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the electrodes and electrolyte interface. In low frequency region, the quasi-vertical line illustrates a typical capacitor behavior [48,49]. The obtained Rs and Rct for CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC composites were 1.06/0.23, 1.13/0.13 and 1.14/0.17 ohm, respectively, which are smaller than bare NOHPC (1.19/0.17 ohm). The slightly higher Rct of CD/NOHPC than other samples may be attributed to its higher SBET and Vpore. In addition, the relationship between impedance phase angle and frequency was used to estimate the electrochemical characteristics of electrode, as shown in Fig. 7b. When the phase angle is − 45°, the corresponding relaxation time is the minimum time needed to discharge all the energy from the device with an efficiency of more than 50%, the smaller indicates the higher power and energy can be transported instantaneous [31,50]. According to formula of
f
0
=
1
τ
0
, of CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC, GO/NOHPC and NOHPC are only 2.9, 2.4, 2.2 and 2.2 ms, respectively. This finding strongly suggests that the NOHPC-based composites exhibited great potentialities for the instantaneous delivery of high power and energy.

Symmetrical all-solid-state supercapacitor devices (SSCs) were assembled using PVA/H2SO4 gel as electrolyte and two as-prepared porous carbon electrodes as work electrode. As shown in Fig. 8a, the CV curves of assembled SSCs exhibited near-rectangular electric double layer capacitance characteristics in the range of voltage 0–1 V. The larger integral area for the CD/NOHPC SSC indicated its higher specific capacitance in all assembled SSCs. Fig. 8b shows the CV curves of CD/NOHPC SSC at different sweep speeds range of 10–200 mVs−1. All curves exhibited a regular rectangle and showed no obvious distortion even at a high scan rate of 200 mV s−1, suggesting an ideal capacitive behavior and a high rate capability. All the GCD curves of the assembled SSCs shown in Fig. 8c display nearly isosceles triangle shape, demonstrating an ideal EDLC capacitance. Fig. 8d and Fig. S4 show the GCD curves of CD/NOHPC SSC and other assembled SSCs at different current densities, respectively. Fig. 8e presents the C
s and C
v plots calculate from the discharge curves. At 1Ag−1, the C
s and C
v of CD/NOHPC SSC were 77.6 F g−1 and 2.33 F cm−3, which is higher than 67.7 F g−1 (2.03 F cm−3) of CNT/NOHPC SSC, 76.3 F g−1 (2.29 F cm−3) of GO/NOHPC SSC and 66.3 F g−1 (1.99 F cm−3) of NOHPC SSC. Moreover, the C
s and C
v of CD/NOHPC SSC were 67.5 A g−1 and 2.03 F cm−3 at a high current density of 20 A g−1, and the corresponding retention was 87%, indicating unexpected rate capability.

To further evaluate the electrochemical properties of the NOHPC SSCs, their Ragone plots of the energy density vs. power density is presented in Fig. 8f. CD/NOHPC SSC delivered the highest energy density of 10.3 Wh kg−1 (0.31 mWh cm−3) at the power density of 489 W kg−1 (14.7 mW cm−3), higher than that of CNT/NOHPC SSC (8.9 Wh kg−1), GO/NOHPC SSC (9.9 Wh kg−1) and NOHPC SSC (8.5 Wh kg−1), and still released an energy density of 3.6 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 5593 W kg−1. Furthermore, the energy density of CD/NOHPC SSC is superior than previously reported SSCs, such as MHPC SSC [51], N-self-doped carbon nanofiber (NCNF) aerogels SSC [52], O/N-co-doped carbon SSC [53], self-doped multi-porous lignin-based biocarbon (SUMBC) SSC [54], hierarchical porous carbons (HPCs) SSC [15], hierarchical N-doped porous carbon nanosheet SSC [55], hierarchical porous carbon SSC [56], porous graphitic biomass carbon (PGBC) SSC [57], nitrogen-doped activated carbon SSC [58], nitrogen-doped hierarchically porous carbon SSC [59].

An ideal electrode material should possess not only good electrochemical capacitance, but also good cycle stability under long-term charge/discharge. The cycling stability of CD/NOHPC SSC was carried out by 20,000 GCD cycles at a current density of 5 mA cm−2. As shown in Fig. 8g, the CD/NOHPC SSC delivered super stability with 109% capacitance retention after 20,000 cycles. The CV curves (Fig. S5) obtained before cycle and after 20,000 cycles, also confirmed the high stability of CD/NOHPC SSC.

The capacitance properties of assembled SSC at different bending angles are presented in Fig. 9. From Fig. 9a, the CV curves of CD/NOHPC SSC at α angle coincided with that at β angle. It demonstrated that CD/NOHPC SSC possessed advantages of good stability and high bending ability. Furthermore, three CD/NOHPC SSCs connected in series can illuminate a red light (Fig. 9b). These results indicate that nanocarbons modification in precursor is an effectively way to regulate the capacitance performance of porous carbon materials.

In summary, three kinds of carbon composites, CD/NOHPC, CNT/NOHPC and GO/NOHPC were obtained by the pyrolysis of nanocarbons modified organic precursor using the silica nanospheres and ZnCl2 as templates. The modification of nanocarbons can effectively improve the hierarchical pore structures, which significantly increases the ion accessible area and accelerates ion diffusion during electrochemical energy storage. Furthermore, the increased N, O doping can provide certain pseudocapacitance and improve the wettability of carbon materials. Among the resulting materials, the as-prepared CD/NOHPC possessed more enhanced chemical and physical features, such as more rational pore distribution and larger specific surface area as well as more effective N, O doping, which is favorable for the electrochemical performance. Our work indicates that the strategy of modification with nanocarbons in organic precursor to regulate the morphology, pore structures, heteroatom doping of carbon materials can open a new approach to design high performance electrode materials for supercapacitors.

Yi Hu: Experimental design, Data curation, Writing - original draft preparation. Jingmiao Cui: Experimental design, Data curation,Writing - original draft preparation. Wansheng Luo: Data curation. Weiliang Zeng: Data curation. Hongying Quan: Experimental design, Writing - review & editing. Dezhi Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 51968049, 52063023) of China, the Youth Science Foundation (Grant No. 20192ACB21031, 20202ACBL204012) of Jiangxi Province, China, the Young Talents Training Plan (Grant No. 20192BCB23012) for Scientific and Technological Innovation of Jiangxi Province, China.
